title: Analyse Heat Engine Cycles
description: Model and compare the thermodynamic cycles of different heat engines (Carnot, Otto, Diesel, Stirling) by computing PV diagrams, work done, and efficiency.
whyItMatters: Heat engines power the modern world — from car engines to power stations. Understanding their thermodynamic cycles reveals why no engine can be 100% efficient and connects abstract thermodynamic laws to real machines.

steps.title: Implement the ideal gas
steps.description: Write functions for isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, and isochoric processes. Calculate P, V, T at each state point.
steps.tips: For an adiabatic process: PV^gamma = constant. For air, gamma = 1.4.

steps.title: Plot the Carnot cycle
steps.description: Compute and plot the PV diagram for a Carnot cycle between two temperatures. Calculate the work done (area enclosed) and efficiency.
steps.tips: Carnot efficiency = 1 - T_cold/T_hot. This is the theoretical maximum.

steps.title: Compare engine cycles
steps.description: Implement Otto (petrol engine), Diesel, and Stirling cycles. Plot all four PV diagrams and compare efficiencies.
steps.tips: The Otto cycle uses two adiabatic and two isochoric processes.

steps.title: Temperature-entropy diagrams
steps.description: Plot T-S diagrams for each cycle. The area enclosed represents the net heat input. Compare this representation to the PV diagram.
steps.tips: On a T-S diagram, the Carnot cycle is a perfect rectangle.

steps.title: Real-world parameters
steps.description: Use realistic parameters for a car engine (compression ratio 10:1, T_hot = 2500K, T_cold = 300K) and compare theoretical efficiency to actual car engine efficiency (~25-30%).
steps.tips: The gap between theoretical and actual efficiency is due to friction, incomplete combustion, and heat losses.

deliverables: PV diagrams for all four cycles
T-S diagrams for all four cycles
Analysis of why real engines fall short of Carnot efficiency
Written report comparing the thermodynamic cycles

rubric.description: All processes and state calculations are physically correct
Clear, well-labelled PV and T-S diagrams
Correct efficiency calculations with comparison to Carnot limit
Meaningful discussion of real-world efficiency losses
